title: Pellaea dichotoma
taxonomy: Pellaea dichotoma is classified within the kingdom Plantae, and more specifically within the phylum Pteridophyta, which includes ferns. It belongs to the family Pteridaceae, commonly known as the wood fern family. The genus Pellaea is a group of ferns that are native to Australia and are characterized by their simple or forked fronds. Pellaea dichotoma is a specific species within this genus. It is further classified within the order Polypodiales and the class Polypodiopsida.
uses: Pellaea dichotoma, also known as the forked fan fern or Australian maidenhair fern, has been used in traditional medicine by the Indigenous Australians. The plant's fronds are said to have been used to treat a variety of ailments, including fever and skin conditions. In some cases, the plant's leaves have been used as a remedy for wounds and cuts. However, the effectiveness and safety of these uses have not been extensively scientifically studied. Further research is needed to confirm the medicinal properties and potential uses of Pellaea dichotoma.

intro: Pellaea dichotoma, also known as Mountain fern or Australian Button Fern, is a species of fern that belongs to the family Pteridaceae. The health benefits of Pellaea dichotoma include its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which may help to reduce inflammation and protect against cell damage, and its potential use in treating respiratory issues and skin conditions. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and saponins, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. Pellaea dichotoma is commonly prepared as a tea, infusion, or tincture, and is also used in traditional medicine, particularly in Australia, where it is valued for its medicinal and cultural significance.
